title: Setting Boundaries: Max Depth
concept: Setting a max depth limits the number of question levels a decision tree is allowed to have, keeping the tree simple and preventing it from building overly specific branches.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How does pruning help a decision tree make better predictions on new data?

options.text: By cutting back overly specific branches that do not help classify new data.
By making the tree twice as deep to capture tiny random outliers.
By converting all classification splits into circular margins.

exercises.explain: Correct! Pruning cuts back the useless twigs and overly specific branches, leaving a clean, simple, and generalizable tree.

cards.title: The 3-Question Limit
cards.body: Imagine you are playing '20 Questions' to guess an animal. If you get 20 questions, you might ask super-specific things like: 'Does it live in a yellow cage in the zoo on Main Street?' But if you are limited to only 3 questions, you must focus on the most important details: 'Is it warm-blooded?', 'Does it fly?', and 'Is it a predator?' Limiting questions forces you to focus on the big picture.

cards.title: What is Max Depth?
cards.body: Max Depth is a boundary setting that limits the maximum number of question levels (the height of the tree from root to leaves) a decision tree is allowed to grow. It acts as a hard ceiling, preventing the tree from asking too many sequential questions.

cards.title: A One-Page Flowchart
cards.body: Imagine writing a decision guide for new employees. If you have infinite paper, you might write a flowchart with 50 levels of questions, accounting for every single weird event. But if you are limited to a single sheet of paper, you can only fit a 3-level flowchart. This forces you to write only the most crucial rules.

cards.title: Predicting Movie Hits
cards.body: A studio predicts if a movie will be a hit. With Max Depth = 2, the tree only asks: 'Is it an action movie?' and 'Does it star a famous actor?'. With Max Depth = 20, the tree might ask: 'Was the main actor's coffee warm on day 12 of filming?' Limiting the depth keeps the questions relevant.

cards.title: Finding the Sweet Spot
cards.body: You might think a lower Max Depth is always better. However, if Max Depth is too low (like 1), the model becomes too simple and misses key patterns (underfitting). If it's too high, it overfits. Machine learning engineers must experiment to find the perfect middle ground.

cards.title: Shallow vs. Deep Trees
cards.body: Comparing different Max Depth settings on a tree:

left.text:
Only 2 questions deep.
Broad, robust rules.
e.g. Size -> Location -> Price.
Easy to read and general.

right.text:
15 questions deep.
Tiny, highly nested boxes.
e.g. Size -> Location -> Color -> Mailbox -> Price.
Overfitted and messy.

exercises.prompt: What is the effect of setting a low 'max depth' on a decision tree?

options.text: It keeps the tree simple by limiting the maximum number of question levels.
It forces the tree to split on every single feature in alphabetical order.
It automatically converts the tree into a curved boundary model.
It increases the number of branches to capture single outlier points.

exercises.explain: Correct! Max depth sets a ceiling on the number of question levels, keeping the tree simple and shallow.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A decision tree with a max depth of 50 is more likely to overfit than a tree with a max depth of 3.
exercises.explain: True! A depth of 50 allows the tree to ask a massive sequence of highly specific questions, which easily leads to memorizing training outliers.

exercises.sentence: Setting a limit on the number of question levels in a decision tree is done by controlling the max ___.
exercises.explain: Max depth restricts how deep the tree's branches can go from the top (root) to the bottom.

exercises.prompt: Which of these decision trees has the most restrictive max depth constraint?

options.text: A tree that is only allowed to ask 2 questions in a row before making a decision.
A tree that is allowed to ask up to 10 questions in a row.
A tree that is allowed to grow until every data point is isolated.

exercises.explain: Correct! Setting the limit to 2 questions is the most restrictive max depth setting, keeping the tree very shallow.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a music recommendation system. Your tree is currently using a max depth of 15 and is making recommendations based on highly specific coincidences like: 'If age > 20 AND timezone is EST AND favorite snack is popcorn -> Recommend classical music.' How should you fix this?

options.text: Reduce the max depth to a smaller value (like 3 or 4) to force the tree to focus only on main features like favorite genre and artist.
Increase the max depth to 30 to allow even more detailed rules.
Delete the entire tree and replace it with a single question asking if they like classical music.
Keep the max depth at 15 but change the training data to only include people who eat popcorn.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Reducing the max depth restricts the tree to the most important questions, preventing it from splitting on coincidences like favorite snacks. Next, let's learn another boundary: minimum samples to split!
